topic: Someone finds out they have a half-sibling they never knew about
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crumpled adoption papers lay on the dusty attic floor, a ridiculous, flimsy thing that had somehow ended up in my hands. I picked them up with the tips of my fingers, a faint, sour taste rising in my throat. Another complication. Another person I was apparently obligated to acknowledge. My jaw tightened, a muscle jumping beneath my skin. This whole discovery felt like an unwelcome intrusion, a smudge on the carefully curated neatness of my life. I reread the name—a name I’d never heard, a face I’d never seen—and a sharp, dismissive laugh escaped me. They were just another piece of inconvenient family history, nothing more. I tossed the papers back onto the floor, the sound sharp in the silence of the old house, and turned my back, focusing instead on the mundane task of cleaning the cobwebs.

[story 2]

When the lawyer called, the words landed like stones in my stomach. A sibling. A ghost from a life I’d never been privy to. I stared at the phone, the plastic cool against my palm. My knuckles were white where I gripped the receiver. There was no surprise, only a profound sense of being inconvenienced. I processed the information with a cold, analytical detachment, cataloging the implications rather than feeling any warmth or shock. "Right," I muttered to the empty room, the sound flat and devoid of inflection. "More people to manage." I hung up abruptly, pacing the length of the living room. Every movement was stiff, controlled, as if I were navigating a minefield. This wasn't a revelation; it was an administrative burden, something to be filed away and ignored.

[story 3]

I found the photograph tucked inside a worn copy of an old novel. It was a smiling face, undeniably familiar, yet utterly alien. A sudden, intense wave of irritation washed over me, so strong it made my skin prickle. I held the picture up to the weak afternoon light, my expression hardening into something severe. How could someone just drop this bombshell? It felt undeserved, an imposition on my solitude. I dropped the photo onto the mahogany desk with a deliberate, heavy thud. My breathing hitched for a moment, a brief flare of annoyance, before I forced it down. There was no joy, no confusion—just a deep-seated feeling that this entire situation was beneath my notice, an unnecessary distraction from whatever important thing I was supposed to be doing.
